sanskrit: श्रद्धया परया तप्तं तपस्तत्ित्रविधं नरैः । अफलाकाङ्क्षिभिर्युक्तैः सात्त्विकं परिचक्षते ॥
hindiTranslation: परम श्रद्धासे युक्त फलेच्छारहित मनुष्योंके द्वारा तीन प्रकारशरीर, वाणी और मन का तप किया जाता है, उसको सात्त्विक कहते हैं ॥
englishTranslation: That threefold discipline of body, speech, and mind, performed with supreme trust and without craving results, is called sattvic.
essence: True discipline is clean only when reward is no longer the motive.
speaker: Krishna
simpleMeaning: Body, speech, and mind are called pure discipline when practiced with trust and without wanting any reward.
simpleInsight: Real discipline feels lighter when it is not bargaining for payoff.
detailedMeaning: On Kurukshetra, Krishna is still answering Arjuna’s crisis by defining real inner discipline. After describing discipline of body, speech, and mind, he says that such practice is sattvic only when it is done with trust and no hunger for reward.
modernRelevance: You keep the promise to train at dawn, then no one sees the effort and the applause never comes. The practice still counts when you were never negotiating for praise.
translationLiteral: श्रद्धया (with trust) / परया (supreme) / तप्तं (performed) / तपः (discipline) / तत् (that) / त्रिविधं (threefold) / नरैः (by people) / अफलाकाङ्क्षिभिः (by those not desiring fruit) / युक्तैः (by those joined) / सात्त्विकं (sattvic) / परिचक्षते (they call)
insight: It shifts discipline from performance for approval to steady practice rooted in trust.

quoteVariants.text: Body, speech, and mind become pure when done without wanting reward.
Threefold discipline. No craving for fruit.
Maybe discipline is clean only when reward stops mattering.
